1: A direct revelation: "Then Pharisees and scribes came to Jesus from Jerusalem and said..." The delegation from Jerusalem — capital city, seat of the Sanhedrin — marks an escalation in the opposition. They come not to learn but to dispute. The Christological frame: Jesus as the authoritative interpreter of Torah is now directly challenged by the institution that claims interpretive authority. The conflict is not merely about handwashing but about who has the right to say what the Torah means — and ultimately, who Jesus is.
2: A direct revelation: "Why do your disciples break the tradition of the elders? For they do not wash their hands when they eat." The charge is about paradosis tōn presbyterōn — the oral tradition of the elders, which the Pharisees held to be an authoritative fence around Torah. Jesus will invert this: the tradition of the elders breaks the commandment of God (Matthew 15:3). The Christological claim is implicit: Jesus' authority exceeds the accumulated authority of all the elders; he speaks as the one who knows the Father's intention directly (Matthew 11:27).
3: A direct revelation: "He answered them, 'And why do you break the commandment of God for the sake of your tradition?'" The counter-question shifts the terrain: the accusation of breaking tradition is answered by the accusation of breaking the commandment of God. Jesus here positions himself as the defender of the Father's actual intent against the human accumulation that distorts it. This is the claim of the one who knows the Father perfectly — not merely an interpreter but the Son who speaks from within the divine mind.
4: A direct fulfillment: "For God commanded, 'Honor your father and your mother,' and 'Whoever reviles father or mother must surely die.'" The citation of Exodus 20:12 and Exodus 21:17 establishes God's commandment as the standard by which the Corban tradition is judged. Jesus speaks as the one who knows what God commanded — with the authority of the giver, not merely the receiver, of the commandment. The appeal to the divine "commanded" versus the human "tradition" is the hermeneutical key to the entire controversy.
5: A direct revelation: "But you say, 'If anyone tells his father or his mother, what you would have gained from me is given to God...'" The Corban ruling allowed a person to declare resources dedicated to the Temple (qorban), which released them from the obligation to support aging parents. Jesus identifies this as a device for voiding the commandment of God under a veneer of piety. The Christological bearing: the one who came to fulfill the Law (Matthew 5:17) exposes the way human tradition has emptied the Law of its content.
6: A direct revelation: "He need not honor his father. So for the sake of your tradition you have made void the word of God." The verb "made void" (ēkyrōsate) is a legal term — annulled, invalidated. Jesus issues a legal ruling against the ruling of the Pharisees. The authority behind this counter-ruling is not precedent or argument from tradition but the direct authority of the Son who knows what the Father commanded. This is the Christological shape of Jesus' teaching throughout: he speaks as one who has authority (Matthew 7:29), not as the scribes.
7: A direct fulfillment: "You hypocrites! Well did Isaiah prophesy of you, when he said..." The citation of Isaiah as prophetically addressed to the Pharisees is a Christological move: Jesus reads the prophetic text as speaking about the present moment. He is the one who can make this identification — the prophet saw this generation's hardness, and Jesus names it. The word "hypocrites" (hypokritai, stage actors) charges them with performing religion while the heart is absent.
8: A direct fulfillment: "'This people honors me with their lips, but their heart is far from me.'" The citation of Isaiah 29:13 (LXX) identifies the Pharisaic practice as the fulfillment of Isaiah's diagnosis of Israel's lip-service religion. The Christological weight: Jesus speaks as the one to whom lips and hearts are directed — "honors me" is the God-directed worship that the Pharisees perform externally while their hearts are absent. The claim is that they honor not merely God in the abstract but the one standing before them, while their hearts reject him.
9: A direct fulfillment: "In vain do they worship me, teaching as doctrines the commandments of men." The Isaiah citation continues: worship becomes vain when it is governed by human commandments rather than divine ones. The Christological application: Jesus is the one who can adjudicate the difference between divine and human commandments because he is the Son who knows the Father's will directly. He quotes Isaiah not as a precedent but as a prophecy of the present — the fulfiller who recognizes the pattern.
10: A revelation of God: "And he called the people to him and said to them, 'Hear and understand:'" The call to hearing and understanding — the refrain of the parable discourse (Matthew 13:9, 43) — now addresses the crowd rather than only the Pharisees. Jesus invites the people to receive his authoritative teaching on what defiles. The Christological posture: he does not retreat to a private setting but speaks publicly and commands understanding. He is the teacher who both demands and enables comprehension.
11: A direct revelation: "It is not what goes into the mouth that defiles a person, but what comes out of the mouth; this defiles a person." This saying overturns the entire logic of food purity in one sentence — a more radical act than the Pharisees' Corban ruling. The Christological authority is immense: Jesus effectively sets aside the food purity laws of Leviticus 11 by a single authoritative declaration. Mark 7:19 notes that Jesus "declared all foods clean." This is the authority of the one who gave the laws in the first place and who now declares their eschatological fulfillment.
12: A revelation of God: "Then the disciples came and said to him, 'Do you know that the Pharisees were offended when they heard this saying?'" The disciples' concern about Pharisaic offense is the pastoral worry of those who must live in religious society. The Christological response (Matthew 15:13-14) reveals that Jesus is not calibrating his teaching to avoid offense — he is saying what is true regardless of its reception. The stone of stumbling (Isaiah 8:14) causes offense precisely because it is real.
13: A direct revelation: "He answered, 'Every plant that my heavenly Father has not planted will be rooted up.'" The Father's planting versus human tradition is the eschatological standard. What the Father has not planted cannot stand — including the Pharisaic system that has replaced divine commandment with human tradition. The Christological claim: Jesus speaks about "my heavenly Father" with the intimacy of the Son (Matthew 11:27), and what the Father plants versus uproots is known to the Son who speaks from within that relationship.
14: A revelation of God: "Let them alone; they are blind guides. And if the blind lead the blind, both will fall into a pit." The blind-guides image is a Christological indictment: the leaders entrusted with guiding Israel to the Messiah cannot see the one standing before them. The blindness is not intellectual but volitional — they have closed their eyes (Matthew 13:15). The Christological irony: the guides who should have recognized and proclaimed the Messiah are the ones who will engineer his death.
15: A revelation of God: "But Peter said to him, 'Explain to us the parable.'" Peter's request for explanation mirrors the disciples' pattern of seeking understanding (Matthew 13:10, 36). The Christological pedagogy continues: the disciples receive the inside track of explanation that the crowds and Pharisees do not. Peter's later confession (Matthew 16:16) comes from a disciple who has been consistently brought inside the teaching that others reject.
16: A revelation of God: "And he said, 'Are you also still without understanding?'" The gentle rebuke — "still without understanding" — is the Christological patience of the teacher who has explained much and finds his closest followers still at the beginning. The word "still" implies expectation: after all they have heard and seen, understanding should be further along. The Christological demand is not merely intellectual assent but transformative comprehension that is still developing in the disciples.
17: A revelation of God: "Do you not see that whatever goes into the mouth passes into the stomach and is expelled?" The physiological explanation — food enters, passes through, exits — grounds the theological principle in common bodily knowledge. The Christological move: Jesus appeals to what everyone knows about the body to expose what the Pharisees have missed about the heart. The teacher who speaks with authority does not disdain ordinary knowledge but uses it to illuminate divine intention.
18: A direct revelation: "But what comes out of the mouth proceeds from the heart, and this defiles a person." The heart as the source of defilement locates the problem of sin at its root. The Christological bearing: the one who teaches about the heart's defilement is the same one who will bear the full weight of human-heart darkness in the passion. The purity of Jesus' own heart — tested in the wilderness, sustained through the ministry — is the contrast to the defilement he diagnoses.
19: A revelation of God: "For out of the heart come evil thoughts, murder, adultery, sexual immorality, theft, false witness, slander." The catalog of vices from the heart is the internal catalog that corresponds to the external commandments (commandments 6-9 of the Decalogue). The Christological bearing: Jesus has already addressed these vices in the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:21-30) as the heart-level interpretation of Torah. The one who taught the heart-standard now explains why the heart is the source of defilement — and why only a new heart (promised in Jeremiah 31:33; Ezekiel 36:26) can resolve the problem.
20: A revelation of God: "These are what defile a person. But to eat with unwashed hands does not defile a person." The closing verdict settles the controversy: ritual handwashing is not a defilement issue; the heart's vices are. The Christological authority: Jesus has adjudicated the question authoritatively, appealing to the Father's commandment over the elders' tradition. The verdict is not subject to appeal — he speaks as the one who knows what the Father intends.
21: A direct revelation: "And Jesus went away from there and withdrew to the district of Tyre and Sidon." The withdrawal to Tyre and Sidon — Gentile territory on the Phoenician coast — is a geographical Christological statement. Jesus moves outside Israel's borders following the Pharisees' opposition. The Gentile territory will produce a faith that the religious leaders of Israel have rejected. The movement from Jewish controversy to Gentile faith is the pattern of the book of Acts, anticipated here.
22: A direct revelation: "And behold, a Canaanite woman from that region came out and was crying, 'Have mercy on me, O Lord, Son of David; my daughter is severely oppressed by a demon.'" The Canaanite woman's cry is a Christological confession from the Gentile margin: "Lord, Son of David" is the full messianic title — lordship and Davidic lineage together. The depth of the irony: the Pharisees from Jerusalem question Jesus' authority; a Canaanite woman recognizes and confesses it. Her cry "have mercy on me" is the prayer of the Psalms (Psalms 41:4, 10; Matthew 51:1) addressed to the Messiah.
23: A revelation of God: "But he did not answer her a word. And his disciples came and begged him, saying, 'Send her away, for she is crying out after us.'" The silence of Jesus — not dismissal but testing — creates the space for the woman's persistent faith to be revealed. The disciples' request to send her away reflects their own ethnic and social boundaries. The Christological drama: the silence is not indifference but the condition in which the genuineness of her faith can emerge. The test of silence is a more profound form of encounter than immediate response.
24: A revelation of God: "He answered, 'I was sent only to the lost sheep of the house of Israel.'" The limitation of Jesus' earthly mission to Israel (cf. Matthew 10:5-6) is stated frankly. The Christological logic: the Son came first to Israel, fulfilling the covenantal priority of salvation (Romans 1:16: "to the Jew first"). This is not rejection of Gentiles in principle but sequential fulfillment of the OT promise — Israel first, then the nations. The Great Commission (Matthew 28:19) will reverse the restriction after the resurrection.
25: A direct revelation: "But she came and knelt before him, saying, 'Lord, help me.'" The woman's prostration and petition — "Lord, help me" — is worship and prayer directed to Jesus. The shortening of the prayer to its essential core ("Lord, help me") is the cry of utter dependence. The Christological bearing: she has heard his statement of limitation (Matthew 15:24) and presses past it, trusting in his mercy beyond the stated boundary. The faith that persists past the apparent refusal is the faith that receives what it seeks.
26: A revelation of God: "And he answered, 'It is not right to take the children's bread and throw it to the dogs.'" The metaphor of children and dogs — Israel and Gentiles in the common Jewish idiom — restates the missionary priority. The Christological tension: Jesus uses the very language that would exclude her to create the opening for her response. The dogs (kynarion, little dogs, household dogs — not the street dogs of insult) are in the house; they are not strangers to the master's table but domestic animals who receive crumbs. The diminutive is merciful.
27: A direct revelation: "She said, 'Yes, Lord, yet even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from their masters' table.'" The woman's response is a masterpiece of faithful argumentation: she accepts the metaphor, accepts her position as a dog, and finds within it the ground of hope. The crumbs from the master's table are more than enough. The Christological bearing: her faith grasps the overflow of the kingdom's abundance — that even the edges of Jesus' ministry are sufficient to heal. She does not ask for Israel's portion but for the residue of mercy.
28: A direct revelation: "Then Jesus answered her, 'O woman, great is your faith! Be it done for you as you desire.' And her daughter was healed instantly." The exclamation "O woman, great is your faith!" (ō gynai, megalē sou hē pistis) is the highest commendation in Matthew — paralleling only the centurion's faith (Matthew 8:10: "not even in Israel have I found such faith"). The immediate healing confirms the commendation. The Christological revelation: the Gentile woman's persistent faith in the Messiah receives what Israel's religious establishment has rejected — the mercy of the Son of David.
29: A revelation of God: "Jesus went on from there and walked beside the Sea of Galilee. And he went up on the mountain and sat down there." The return to the Galilean lakeside and the mountain setting mirrors the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:1) and the Feeding of the 5000 (Matthew 14:23). Jesus sits in the teacher's posture. The mountain in Matthew is consistently the locus of divine encounter: teaching (Matthew 5:1), prayer (Matthew 14:23), transfiguration (Matthew 17:1), commission (Matthew 28:16). The mountain-sitting signals that a significant revelation follows.
30: A revelation of God: "And great crowds came to him, bringing with them the lame, the blind, the crippled, the mute, and many others, and they put them at his feet, and he healed them." The catalog of healings — lame, blind, crippled, mute — is the fulfillment of Isaiah 35:5-6 ("the eyes of the blind shall be opened, and the ears of the deaf unstopped; the lame man shall leap like a deer, and the tongue of the mute shall sing for joy"). The messianic healing agenda is systematically fulfilled. The crowds who put the sick "at his feet" are in the posture of worship before the healer.
31: A direct fulfillment: "So that the crowd marveled, when they saw the mute speaking, the crippled healthy, the lame walking, and the blind seeing. And they glorified the God of Israel." The glorification of "the God of Israel" by the crowd echoes the setting (Gentile territory) and the sequence: the messianic healings in Gentile regions cause Gentiles to glorify the God of Israel. This is the fulfillment of Isaiah 42:6-7 (the servant as "a light for the nations, to open eyes that are blind"). The healer brings Gentile worship to the God of Israel through his works.
32: A revelation of God: "Then Jesus called his disciples to him and said, 'I have compassion on the crowd because they have been with me now three days and have nothing to eat. And I am not willing to send them away hungry, lest they faint on the way.'" The compassion statement parallels Matthew 14:14 (the feeding of the 5000); the three-day period mirrors the wilderness wandering's food crisis. The Christological bearing: Jesus does not send away the hungry. "I am not willing to send them away hungry" is the divine refusal to abandon the needy — echoing the divine shepherd of Ezek 34 who does not scatter the flock but feeds it.
33: A revelation of God: "And the disciples said to him, 'Where are we to get enough bread in such a desolate place to feed so great a crowd?'" The disciples' question — after already having witnessed the feeding of the 5000 (Matthew 14:19) — reveals the limits of their comprehension. The same "desolate place" (erēmia), the same insufficiency, the same crowd: yet they ask where bread will come from. The Christological patience: Jesus answers the question of inadequate resources with the same action as before, teaching the disciples by repetition that his provision is not exhausted by one miracle.
34: A revelation of God: "And Jesus said to them, 'How many loaves do you have?' They said, 'Seven, and a few small fish.'" The inventory of inadequacy is again precise: seven loaves, a few small fish. The Christological pattern repeats: bring what you have to me, and I will multiply it. The number seven (unlike the five of Matthew 14:17) has symbolic resonance — seven is the number of completeness in the OT. The feeding of the 4000 mirrors the feeding of the 5000, with variations that suggest both events are real and distinct.
35: A revelation of God: "And directing the crowd to sit down on the ground." The command to sit (anapesein, to recline — the table-fellowship posture) is the same as the feeding of the 5000 (Matthew 14:19). The reclining crowd is placed in the posture of the eschatological banquet (Matthew 8:11: "many will recline with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob in the kingdom of heaven"). The Christological host provides not a standing meal but a feast of rest — the same abundance of the messianic banquet enacted in ordinary bread and fish.
36: A type: "He took the seven loaves and the fish, and having given thanks he broke them and gave them to the disciples, and the disciples gave them to the crowds." The eucharistic formula — took, gave thanks, broke, gave — is identical to 14:19 and the Last Supper (Matthew 26:26-27). The threefold repetition of the eucharistic action (5000, 4000, Last Supper) weaves the feeding miracles together as anticipations of the table where Christ gives himself. The disciples as distributors are the mediation-pattern of the church's ministry.
37: A type: "And they all ate and were satisfied. And they took up seven baskets full of the broken pieces left over." The seven baskets (as opposed to twelve in Matthew 14:20) may correspond to the seven nations of Canaan (Deuteronomy 7:1) or the seventy nations of Gen 10, signaling the Gentile dimension of the feeding. The superabundance — seven baskets of leftovers after all were satisfied — is the signature of divine provision: more than enough, with surplus. The kingdom's table does not run short.
38: A type: "Those who ate were four thousand men, besides women and children." The four thousand (plus women and children — a larger total than the named figure) in Gentile territory parallels the five thousand in Jewish territory. Together the two feedings span both peoples. The Christological scope: Jesus feeds both Jewish and Gentile crowds in the wilderness — the new Moses provision extends to all nations before the Great Commission makes it explicit.
39: A revelation of God: "And after sending away the crowds, he got into the boat and went to the region of Magadan." The departure by boat follows the pattern of Matthew 14 (withdrawal after feeding, sea crossing). The region of Magadan (possibly Magdala/Dalmanutha on the western shore of Galilee) is the transition point between the Gentile mission and the return to Jewish controversy (Matthew 16:1: Pharisees and Sadducees demanding a sign). The geography of Jesus' ministry traces the kingdom's expanding circle: Jewish controversy, Gentile healing and feeding, return to Jewish questioning.
